![Thumbnail (1920x1080)](https://i.ytimg.com/vi/087spL8hMvM/maxresdefault.jpg)
# [How to Self-Host LLMs and Multi-Modal AI Models with NVIDIA NIM in 5 Minutes](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=087spL8hMvM)

**Visibility**: Public
**Uploaded by**: [NVIDIA Developer](https://www.youtube.com/@NVIDIADeveloper)
**Uploaded at**: 2024-07-29
**Published at**: 
**Length**: 04:10
**Views**: 48665
**Likes**: 674
**Category**: Science & Technology

## Description

```
-NVIDIA NIM is containerized AI inference software that makes it simple to deploy production-ready model endpoints accelerated by NVIDIA GPUs, anywhere you need them. Tap into the latest AI foundation models—like NVIDIA Nemotron, Qwen, DeepSeek R1, Meta Llama, and more— ready for secure, private deployment in 5 minutes or less on NVIDIA-accelerated workstations, datacenter or cloud environments. 

Join Neal Vaidya, developer advocate at NVIDIA, for a demo on how to privately deploy LLMs and multi-modal AI models with NVIDIA NIM This tutorial focuses on running Llama 3 locally with NIM, but once you’re up and running with NIM, it’s easy to tap into NVIDIA Nemotron, Qwen, DeepSeek, Mistral, Meta, and more—all with the same simple workflow.

0:22 - Overview of NIM microservices (https://nvda.ws/4bZLY9E)
0:36 - Test the NVIDIA-hosted NIM endpoint for Llama 3
0:51 - Generate an API key and access sample code for OpenAI API-compatible chat completion endpoints
0:59 - Get instructions for pulling the NIM docker container to run Llama 3 locally
1:22 - How to log-in and authenticate with the NVIDIA NGC private registry from your local environment using the command line (CLI)
1:55 - Create and set an environment variable called NGC_API_Key
2:05 -  Input a single ‘Docker run’ command to pull the NIM container, automatically download optimized model weights, and launch a local LLM endpoint2:19 - Explanation of Docker command options: Expose all GPUs to the running container
2:28 - Explanation of Docker command options: Expose the API key environment variable
2:35 - Explanation of Docker command options Mount the cache to download and store model weights to avoid redownload on future deployments
2:48 - Explanation of Docker command options: Specify the NIM should run as the local user
2:53 - Explanation of Docker command options: Expose the HTTP requests  port to interact with the locally running NIM
3:03 - Explanation of Docker command syntax: Specifying the model name in the container image path
3:30 -  Check that the Llama 3 inference service is running by sending a curl request to the API readiness health check endpoint in another terminal
3:41 - Use curl to send another inference request to the local Llama 3 NIM API endpoint

Developer resources

▶️ Learn more about NIM: https://nvda.ws/472hzbF 
▶️ Join the NVIDIA Developer Program: https://nvda.ws/3OhiXfl
▶️ Trial and download NIM and NVIDIA Blueprints— reference workflows for AI agent sample apps—on the NVIDIA API catalog: https://nvda.ws/4bZLY9E
▶️ Read the Mastering LLM Techniques series to learn about inference optimization including continuous batching, KV caching, model quantization, tensor parallelism and more: https://resources.nvidia.com/en-us-large-language-models

#selfhosting #LLM #nvidianim #aimodel #docker #generativeai #modeldeployment #aiinference #containerizedinference #llmapi #developer #inferenceoptimization #productiongenai #devops #artificialintelligence
```

## Transcript

Looking to get your hands on the latest generative AI
models accelerated by NVIDIA?
Today, we're going to show you how easy it is to try out
and deploy the latest AI models for high-performance GPU-accelerated
inferencing, privately and securely on your own infrastructure
using a single command.
First, we can find out what models are available on the NVIDIA
API catalog at build.nvidia.com.
There are several models available across a bunch of different
domains, like Stable Diffusion and esmfold, but the one we'll
be using today is Llama 3.
We can quickly test out the model right here on our web
browser and see how it performs.
For example, let's try asking the model to write a limerick
on GPU computing.
And here's the resulting output.
We can also test out the model programmatically with the
hosted APIs, by generating an API key and using the sample
code snippets here.
To deploy a NIM ourselves, we can move over to the Docker
tab on this page.
That will give us the instructions for pulling the NIM container and
running it in our own environment.
Note that in order to download NIM, you'll need to sign
up for an account.
You can sign up with a business email for access to NIM through
an NVIDIA AI Enterprise license or a personal email for free
access to NIM through the NVIDIA Developer Program.
You can choose the best option for your needs.
Let's go over to the command line and see what the deployment
process looks like.
The first thing we'll need to do is log into the NVIDIA
Container Registry.
To do this, we'll need to have Docker Engine and the Docker
Command Line tool pre-installed.
We can type in docker login, followed by nvcr.io for NVIDIA
Container Registry.
For the username, we'll use$ oauthtoken, and for the password,
we'll paste in the API key that we got from the API Catalog webpage.
Now, we're authenticated with the Container Registry.
Our next step will be to create an environment variable called
NGC API key in our session that holds the same API key value.
I've already done this, so I'll skip that step here.
And then to get things running, we just need to input the
single docker run command, which I can paste in here.
This will pull the container image, download the necessary
model weights, and deploy the NIM microservice that
we can use as a local API endpoint.
There's a couple of flags here that I think are useful
to take a look at.
The first is GPUsAll.
This is telling Docker to expose all of the GPUs to
the running container.
In the next one, we're letting Docker know to expose the
API key environment variable that we set previously.
Here, we're mounting this path to opt-nim.cache directory
within the Nim container.
This is where Nim will download and store the model weights
so that they don't have to be redownloaded on future deployments.
Next, we're specifying that the NIM should run as our local user.
Finally, here we're exposing the port 8000 from inside
the container to the outside.
That's the main port where we'll be sending all of our
HTTP requests in order to interact with the running NIM.
And the last bit here is the actual container image, the
image path and the tag.
Note here that when we deploy Nim, we include the name of
the model in the image path itself.
So in this case, we'll be deploying the Llama3 8b model, and if
we were going to deploy the Llama3 70b model, or any other model,
it'd be a different image path.
We could scroll then to the end of this log here, and
see that this last message is telling us that our service
is running on port 8000, and that it's ready to listen for requests.
To confirm that the service is ready, I can move over
to another terminal and send this health ready request with curl.
And I see the response come back with the message that
the service is ready.
So now that the NIM is ready, let's try sending a request.
I'll try asking for a limerick again by pasting in this
command to my terminal.
And we quickly get back a response with a lovely little poem, just
like we saw on the web interface.
And that's a wrap.
We've deployed one of the latest community AI
models for secure, high-performance inferencing on our own
NVIDIA-accelerated infrastructure.
Looking to run more NIM examples?
Check out the Jupyter Notebook and resources in the description.

# Harnessing NVIDIA NIM: Unlocking High-Performance AI Deployment Across Domains and Infrastructures

[![Vamsi Doddy](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fill:32:32/1*5mWotYNGc8Y9jk9_zR5-ww.jpeg)](https://medium.com/@vdoddy?source=post_page---byline--fd32132ab854---------------------------------------)

[Vamsi Doddy](https://medium.com/@vdoddy?source=post_page---byline--fd32132ab854---------------------------------------)

[Listen](https://medium.com/m/signin?actionUrl=https%3A%2F%2Fmedium.com%2Fplans%3Fdimension%3Dpost_audio_button%26postId%3Dfd32132ab854&operation=register&redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fmedium.com%2F%40vdoddy%2Fharnessing-nvidia-nim-unlocking-high-performance-ai-deployment-across-domains-and-infrastructures-fd32132ab854&source=---header_actions--fd32132ab854---------------------post_audio_button------------------)

Share

**NVIDIA NIM (NVIDIA Inference Microservices)** is a platform designed to simplify the deployment and optimization of AI models in various environments, including workstations, cloud, and on-premises data centers.

NIM is designed for flexibility and control, enabling seamless model deployment across diverse environments, including local workstations, on-premises data centers, and cloud platforms. Supported infrastructures include **NVIDIA DGX**, **NVIDIA DGX Cloud**, **NVIDIA Certified Systems**, and **NVIDIA RTX** workstations and PCs.

Prebuilt containers and Helm charts, equipped with optimized models, are validated and benchmarked across a range of NVIDIA hardware platforms, cloud providers, and Kubernetes distributions. This ensures compatibility across all NVIDIA-powered environments, enabling organizations to deploy generative AI applications seamlessly, while retaining complete control over their applications and the data they handle.

NIM addresses the need for domain-specific solutions and optimized performance through several advanced features. It includes domain-specific NVIDIA CUDA libraries and customized code designed for areas like language, speech, video processing, healthcare, and more. This tailored approach ensures that applications achieve high accuracy and relevance for their intended use cases.

**Key Features:**

1. NIM containers can run on local RTX-powered workstations or in the cloud, providing versatility for AI development.
2. Optimized for low-latency and high-efficiency inference tasks, NIM delivers significant cost and performance benefits.
3. Developers can run inference locally to avoid data privacy issues associated with cloud-only solutions.
4. Supports fine-tuned models and domain-specific adaptations for use cases like customer support chatbots, RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) systems, and generative AI applications​.

**How It Differs from Other Frameworks:**

- NIM offers more control over data and costs by enabling self-hosted or hybrid deployment models. It eliminates reliance on external APIs, reducing latency and API costs​.
- Unlike more generalized frameworks, NIM includes pre-configured setups for specific AI applications like interactive avatars, speech recognition, and detailed customer engagement models​

**Advantages of using NIM:**

NIM allows deploying AI models on local NVIDIA-powered GPUs or hybrid setups combining on-premises and cloud environments. This avoids sending sensitive data to external cloud servers and utilizes existing hardware infrastructure instead of relying on pay-as-you-go cloud compute costs.

While AWS offers GPU-backed services like **SageMaker** or GCP’s **Vertex AI** Platform supports NVIDIA GPUs, their general-purpose nature may not achieve the same level of hardware efficiency.

NIM includes AI models tailored for tasks like RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation), generative AI, and real-time applications (e.g., customer service avatars or gaming AI).

AWS and GCP typically offer general-purpose AI frameworks, requiring additional customization for domain-specific tasks.

With NIM, we can run inference locally, which eliminates variable cloud compute costs. This is a better choice for workloads requiring sustained performance without the need for frequent scaling up or down, which cloud services excel at. Local inference avoids compliance issues with cloud-based processing of sensitive data (e.g., in regulated industries like healthcare or finance). AWS and GCP address security with advanced features (e.g., AWS Nitro enclaves or GCP’s Confidential Computing) but still involves sending data to cloud-managed resources.

**Situations Favoring AWS or GCP:**

1. **Scalability**:

· AWS (e.g., **Lambda, SageMaker**) and GCP (e.g., **Vertex AI**) provide auto-scaling for inference workloads.

· This is preferable for unpredictable traffic spikes or highly dynamic workloads.

1. **Ease of Integration**:

· AWS and GCP offer comprehensive ecosystems for storage (e.g., S3, GCS), data pipelines, and DevOps integration.

· They’re better suited for teams that need seamless integration across diverse services.

1. **Pre-Built Services for General AI**:

· AWS and GCP provide APIs for pre-trained AI services (e.g., **AWS Comprehend**, **GCP Natural Language**), enabling rapid development for generic use cases.

**Choosing Between NIM, AWS, and GCP:**

- Use **NIM** when:

o You have access to NVIDIA GPUs on-premises.

o Your application demands low-latency, privacy-focused, or domain-optimized AI.

o Cost predictability and data control are the top priorities.

- Choose **AWS or GCP** when:

o You need elastic scalability and seamless multi-service integration.

o Your workload frequently changes or requires global availability.

o You prefer managed services to minimize operational overhead.

**Use Cases:**

- Rapidly build applications requiring real-time responses, such as chatbots or dynamic content generators. Eg: Using NIM to deploy language models fine-tuned for customer support chatbots or blog content generation, leveraging pre-built containers optimized for NVIDIA GPUs.
- AI for specific industries like healthcare, finance, or media. Eg: Detecting anomalies in MRI scans etc.
- Analyze video feeds to detect suspicious activity in real-time using NVIDIA RTX workstations and NIM video processing libraries.
- Use NIM to deploy ASR (Automatic Speech Recognition) models tailored for customer service, enabling accurate and rapid transcription of customer calls.
- Use NIM to deploy vision-based AI models for obstacle detection and navigation.
- Combine local hardware for data retrieval with cloud-based inference for scaling high-performance AI solutions.
- Enhance gaming experiences with real-time AI NPCs or interactive environments​.​
